speaker: interviewer
text: Can we just go back a bit to when you have first arrived in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your first impression?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no impression. We were holding on to-- virtually to each other's hands. We were frightened of the shadows, even in England. When we arrived in England, we sort of began to associate, like, with friends. And at first when we arrived in the hostels, they used to put food on the table. We used to fill our pockets up with white bread. We haven't seen white bread for years. We used to behave like animals, I realize now. We used to-- we never fought or anything like that. But it was that-- we were still under the shadow of what we went through. We were still frightened there won't be any tomorrow or later. And I'm ashamed to say we were like animals in some way. Gradually, you realize that you don't have to do that. You-- it-- it's-- the food is there. There's no shortage, and so on and so on. And we gradually sort of eased off from filling our pockets up with bread or whatever was going. And I was in this hostel, as I said. About six weeks, I was in a hostel in Southampton. Then we were taken to Scotland, traveled hours. It sounded-- for, like, 15 hours, them days. I'm going--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train. We got on the train in Southampton to London. We changed in London. We traveled a whole night to Glasgow. And then another local train-- it seemed like take for ages. We arrived in this hostel, which was called-- and Cardross was the village. [? Dulles ?] House was-- the place was called [? Dulles ?] House. There was already a group of boys and girls who came from Theresienstadt to Windermere. And from Windermere, they closed up that place. And we all met up together in this [? Dulles ?] House. [? Dulles ?] House-- I was there almost all summer. It was a very nice time because, as I was mentioning before, we had plenty to eat. We didn't have to do anything special. We-- some had lessons. Some stayed for lessons. And some went off for the day. We went visiting Loch Lomond. I remember even seeing-- from our bedroom, I can see the liners coming in for service in the-- the-- old Q2-- Q-- and used to come to this place, Helensburgh, for servicing. And we had a lovely time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to classes?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Maybe I-- once or twice. Because, as I said, I was very quick. And I was little. And I was-- I mean, I always-- finishing my breakfast quick, first. And I run down and got one of these bikes, and so on. But gradually, as we were sort of becoming more accustomed to people, to life, we finished up going to the cinema on our own to Dumbarton. And sometimes, the girls were waiting outside with tickets. They used to take us in. People were very kind. Everywhere we were, people were kind. People were nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a big Jewish community there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- where we were, there was none. But in Glasgow, there was. But we was miles from Glasgow because we were, as I said, in this little place near Helensburgh. I had a very-- we had a very good time, all of us, I believe. Because I've got photos from there, and all the rest of it. And gradually, they closed that place up. And I-- we were finished up. Then we're taken to Burnley. I was in a hostel in Burnley over the winter. And I remember it was big snows. And there was-- Burnley is not a small town. But it's not a big town, either. And we used to-- sort of-- we made friends. I mean, the people used to come out of the shops and talk to us. A little bit of talking we could do in English. The best-- I remember, for example, the first joke I ever got. We were taken to a amateur show. And this girl walks on the stage. And she passes out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a guy runs on the stage. And he says, what should I do? Shall I rub her forehead? They said, yes, rub her forehead. He rubs her forehead. She's still out. He said, shall I rub her back? Yes, rub her back. And he-- 'rub her' this and 'rub her' that. Eventually, a bloke walks on with about half a dozen balloons-- 'rubber balloons, rubber balloons.'

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] That's my first joke-- [LAUGHTER] --I got, hundreds of others since. That was when I began to realize that I'm picking up the lingo a little. Whilst-- whilst in Scotland, I might add, I fell off my bike. And I broke my wrist. And I fell off the stairs, and I broke my ankle. When I was-- when I had my ankle in plaster about-- my leg in plaster for about six weeks, they took me to the hospital. And I said, Enugh, enugh, [INAUDIBLE]-- [INAUDIBLE] off-- take off! Take off! I didn't know that E-N-U-G-H means enough. I should know that a G-H is an F [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And that was that. And from there, gradually, as I said, we got into our stride. We were divided. Because Burnley was a small place. Some of us were taken to Burnley, others to Manchester. And they closed Burnley. And we finished up in London. And they mostly found-- they found accommodation for us with women and kids who lost their husbands in the war, overly-- all Jewish. The Jewish Suffragette Committee paid about three pounds a week for food, board. And by then, I had already been-- found a job in Hadley Garden. I was working for a-- a company called Gilded Jewelers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who found you the job?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the Jewish Suffragette Committee. In fact, they sent me and my friend, Bernard, who was an-- who lived in Australia for the last 40 years, which I went-- my wife and I went to see him about-- on our 40th wedding anniversary. And I reminded him. He stayed there. They made good jewelry. And he was making diamond rings and all that. And I was making the cheap jewelry-- gold, but cheap jewelry. And about a month before he left Australia, I got him a job at my place because I was earning double what he was. Because I was making charms and lockets and earrings, all gold. But I had to make so many dozen a day. So I was sort of well above him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took him to our place. And he worked in our place four or six weeks. And had a few pounds in his pocket before he went off to Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And gradually-- as I said, gradually, we started going out, cinema, dancing, met girls. And I met my wife in the Astoria in Charing Cross Road. And we were-- I was living in Willesden at the time. She was living in Tottenham. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did she come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: --not easy. She was a-- a Tottenham-Stamford Hill girl. She was born in England. Some of our-- some of our boys married girls who were also from the-- the camps. And gradually, we got engaged. And then sort of a bad-- and by the way, I was waiting for a visa, Romanian visa to go to America because I was going to go out to my uncle and aunt. By the time my turn came to get this visa, I was already engaged to my wife. And I told them, forget it. And in 1950, we got married. And I have four children, two girls and two boys. I knew how to do it because I got a Helen and a David and a Kim and a Laurie, all in turn. And we had a very, very happy life. Hard work-- I was in piecework for many years. So I got paid per article what I was doing, whether it was making a little stage coach, or whether it was making Tower Bridge or any of these little charms and things. And I was working very hard. I used to work 12, 14 hours a day. I was thinking-- at one time, I was thinking every woman in the country was wearing a pair of my gold earrings-- gypsy earrings I was making. And slowly I was building myself up. I was living with my in-laws to begin with. And then I saved about 500, 600 pounds. My wife was working for the We saved up. And we came down to-- this way, which is Southgate. And we bought a little house. And my little-- my first little house cost me 2,600 pounds. Sounds like a fortune [LAUGHS] at the time. But today, you can't buy a motorbike for it. [LAUGHTER] And I moved on to about-- fourth houses is what I am now. And each one is in the same area. And we brought-- we worked hard. We brought-- we brought up our kids. And we had holidays mostly in Israel because all I had to do is find my fare and stayed with my brothers and sisters, and all that. In fact, my record is, I think, about 11 nights. I slept in-- in a different house every night because everybody insisted-- cousins and nephews and-- that I have to go and stay with them. That was my record.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we used to sleep on the tables because they had little-- little, tiny flats then. My sister had a little tiny flat. And there I-- was I, arriving six of us. What's she going to do? We-- the kids slept on the kitchen tables and on the floorboards, [LAUGHS] almost, and all the rest of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I said, I was married to my darling wife for extremely short 45 years. Unfortunately, she passed away just six months ago. And I feel very lonely and very lost although I've got four lovely kids. Three of them are married. I've got three granddaughters. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My two eldest granddaughters belong to Helen, my first daughter. One was born in Zambia. One was born in-- in Cape Town.

speaker: interviewer
text: We went one Christmas to Cape Town to see them. There was this little girl, blond and blue eyes, running behind me. Hello, Granddad. What you doing, Granddad? And I didn't even know she was talking to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, I says to her, Nicky, call me Uncle Steve. I'm not used to 'Granddad.' She says, I don't want an Uncle Steve. I want a granddad. And that's how we realized that we were sort of getting the same as the rest of the population, the rest of people. We worked hard. We had holidays. We then sort of-- we joined the 45 Aid Society. And from there, life got easier. Because some of the boys who were not doing too well were helped in many ways. Like, for instance, when we went to celebrate 40 years liberation, the-- we booked three rooms, especially for those who couldn't afford to pay. And we are still very, very strong. We feel like we are brothers and sisters. But time takes its toll. As I said before, from a photo of 12 of us, four of them passed away. And one of them is my wife. And I'm very low at the present time. I've got my stone setting on the 1st of September.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what can I say? Life goes on. I am just devastated because I can laugh and tell a joke one minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: And next minute, a handbag of my wife's will fall out. And I look inside, and 50p will be in it. And that's typical Julie. She was happy with 50p in her pocket. And-- and I started crying like a baby. And it's hitting me very hard. I belong to groups now-- Jack's. And we go to-- I go one evening a week to Sobell Center for counseling. And I think to myself, I feel bad about it. When I get there, I see people are much worse off than me. So I come away feeling not too bad. And now I'm planning to go to my family in Israel and spend a few weeks with them. I'm taking-- they're making a wedding. So I'm taking-- some of the children are coming with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what can I say? I hope I said the right thing. I hope I've put my-- as best as I could. I memorized everything I could.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the way, I might-- I might also add. And the last do we had was 51 years from liberation. And we got-- our chairman is Sir Martin Gilbert. We all-- he asked us all to write, if we wanted to, a history of our experiences. I did write a 12- or 14-page one. And a book is coming out in this autumn. 173 of us gave our history, some two pages, some 32 pages. And I'm looking forward to read-- although I know most of their experiences. But what is in words is not the same as what you hear. And now I'm recording my life history, which I've just done. And I hope that, in years to come, that it helps somebody. Or somebody will pick it up when I'm not around anymore and read a little bit of my history. To the best of my ability and the best of my knowledge, it's 100% correct what I-- what I was saying on this tape. And I thank you for giving me the chance to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all-- that's all I can say. Probably I've missed a lot of things out. If I have, I'm sorry. Because it's a long time, and memory is fading.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some mornings-- some days, I-- I says to my wife's photo-- I says, you're laughing at me, Julie, aren't you? Because she always used to say, one of these days, you lose your head. And I-- I forget what I went in the room for, and so on and so on. And that's-- that's life. Life goes on, as they say. [SIGHS] I-- I-- I-- I thought I had enough, what with-- with losing my parents and losing all my family in the war. But I was too young to realize what was happening. This has affected me more [SOBBING] by losing my wife than anything else. We were very good together, brother and-- almost like brother and sister, like mom and dad, and everything else. And I miss her very much. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's terrible. She-- unfortunately, she had cancer all over her body, in the lungs, in the bone marrow. And then she caught pneumonia and shingles. She died at the hospice on November, November 1995. And I miss her very much. And I'm very lonely, yes, a lot of times. That's all I can say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a message or something you would like to leave the future generation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would just like to add that when they-- if they-- and if-- when they see this film of mine, as I already said, to the best of my knowledge and ability, it's 100% what I remember. Maybe in some cases, it's a little bit fuzzy. And I hope one day when I'm gone, that people will see [SOBBING] what happened to our families [SOBBING] and what was going on. [SOBBING] And today, they don't believe it. They say it's a fallacy. They say it's not true. They say it's made up by the-- by the Jewish people. And it's made up. And it's not true, and all the rest of it. One day I phoned up to-- I never phone. I phoned up one of the stations. And there was this guy talking about it's not true. He writes books. I forget his name. I should take a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewer
text: I was so angry. I phoned up. I said, how can you give him airtime? How can you allow a man like that to be on radio and tell these lies? He said to me, Steve, would you like us not to have other-- just other people's point of view, so you can come in and say your side? I said, I would like-- I would not like-- I don't like it because he is there. And you are giving him time. And you know and millions of people know that he's telling lies because it was all fabricated. I know a lot of it was made up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was very angry and annoyed. And I managed to get through to them. And then I had to-- I said-- I started crying. I had to hang up. And that is so. And they allow these people to come along. And, as I said, I hope that in future generations, if only one or two people happen to come across this tape I'm making and it-- it-- and it takes them a little-- makes them feel a little the way my family and I have felt for the last 51 years, [SOBBING] it will help.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, that is one of the reasons we're doing this. And thank you for giving us your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the best of my ability, I told the truth. I might have forgotten to put things in. I might have forgotten one or two-- one or two dates-- not the dates, and all that. But it's 51 years. And it's a long time. It's more than 51-- it's 53 years since they've taken us away. I was only just 13 and 1/2, 14-- excuse me. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know. I don't know. They used to talk about, was there a god? I went to one meeting at the Sobell. And there was a Reverend Hartman. And he was telling them answers, something like, there was a meeting in Auschwitz between X amount of Jews. And they had a meeting, like a court. Is there a god? Or-- or is there not? By the time they finished their meeting, they decided there was no god.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a half an hour later, he was saying, this Hartman, Reverend Hartman, it was time to daven minyan. I don't believe a word of it. And nor-- nor do any of my friends. You had no chance of davening minyan. You had no chance of meeting somebody come along. There is a million-- I won't say no chance. Maybe a million to one that such a situation did take place, but unlikely. Because if it wasn't for me-- you can ask a thousand other people. And they will each one turn and tell you. There was no courts. There was no sit-in. There was no minyans. And there was no-- all that what this particular person told us about. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What can I say? There is lots and lots of things I probably forgot to say. There's lots of things I should have said. And once this tape is finished, I'll be sorry I didn't. But unfortunately, it's a long time. And we do forget things.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I'm glad that I did have the chance to make this tape and tell certain things about the matzevah in Israel and about the situation with my friends here. And we're losing them slowly, unfortunately. And we feel it. We feel it because we are like a big family of brothers and sisters. When we made bar mitzvahs or weddings, we had no family to ask. It was always the same people, our boys and girls, and the same thing with them. And gradually, as we was getting older, we had our own families, and so on and so on. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we are-- now, when someone is ill or somebody dies, we all rush to hospitals. We all rush to see them. We all rush. I feel it like-- also I feel bad, and I felt bad about losing my wife. I feel almost as bad when this, recently, only two weeks ago, a very good friend of mine, Frank [? Farkash, ?] died. And then a week before that, another friend of mine, Manny, Manny Barnard died. And then we had Bernard, Bernard [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Dreihorn. And, and there is one or two who are still ill. And it makes you feel-- the phone goes. Suddenly, everybody rings everybody to say, oh, by the way, he is in hospital. And that's the hospital. And it-- it's-- it's-- it's like I'm losing my own flesh and blood. Because we were brought up since we were 14, 15, 16. We came over here. We were like that. [POINTS] No, I can't explain it to you. That is a bond between us no other people in the world have, not even in Israel. In Israel, they're spread out. There's lots of survivors. There was lots of survivors there. And they-- they-- they don't even like talking about it because they brought their own problems with the wars and all that stuff. But here, we are immune from what they've got in Israel. And we feel more attached to one another. And it's-- it's just-- it's terrible. It's just terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, lately, twice before I went to simcha, the phone goes. Steve, there is a levaya tomorrow-- twice. And it's just-- it's just-- it's just 13. We used to-- we used to make an evening and worry, who can we leave out? Because you didn't want to upset anybody. Now, you-- you start saying, who can we ask? Unfortunately, that is life. I know in every other community, there is also sort of people who know people who are passing away, and all that. But we've seemed-- some feel it more because we are so close to one another.

speaker: interviewee
text: It doesn't matter whether he is in Israel or Manchester, or whatever. One of our boys has passed away. One of our boys is ill. One of our girls has gone to hospital, and so on and so on. Yeah. And I-- I am the type of person who talks to everybody, is not broiled with anybody. I-- I feel very much when something happens not only to my friends themselves, but any-- any of their children, and anything. And we just rally around together. You should have seen it when we done this Torah in the [INAUDIBLE] shul. We're looking forward to having this plaque put up. And it's-- it's-- it's lovely. We don't have enough of these simchas lately. It's-- it's-- there's-- there's this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And unfortunately, my wife was one of them. I think she was only 64 when she died, and it was much too young. She was a lovely person. All I've got to do-- I meet-- I meet a person who just met her five minutes, said, Julie, one in a million. And-- and tears come to my eyes because everybody feels the same about her. She would never row with anybody. She would always agree with everybody. She was-- you just don't meet a person like that. And unfortunately, she's passed away. And her mother only died three and a half months before she did. She lived to 87, the old girl. See, and that's how life is. And I think she's gone too soon, too quick. Because you know what they say-- [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: They say, only the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Only the--

speaker: interviewer
text: --good die young.

speaker: interviewee
text: --good ones die young. That means I'm not. [LAUGHTER] But I try my hardest. I try my best. I-- I belong to the Reformed Shul in Southgate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only this week, the rabbi phoned me. And he said, could you come on Saturday morning at 9 o'clock? I want you to talk to a few of the youth, the bar mitzvah boys and those who are attending, to become bar mitzvahed.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in our case-- in our shul, they have to sign a paper that they're going to attend till 16. Otherwise, he won't bar mitzvah them. So I'm going on Saturday to speak to about a dozen of them who are coming up to their confirmation. And I did it once before. And it was-- they were-- I was very pleasantly surprised that the young people were interested. It was just the fact that they had to do it. They were really interested. And also, I've been going to places like-- as far as Southampton, speaking to schools about my experience. They show them a little film at the beginning-- at first. And then the kids ask questions. And they brought schools from all around that area, you know. I normally go with Liam Rosenberg. I used to go with Bernard Dreihorn. And he's passed away. I go with Charlie Shane. And we go in groups of two. But I also don't know whether any-- any of my friends added the fact that it took two-- it took a family of non-Jewish people to make the first Holocaust Memorial in Nottingham for us. And it was very, very nice.
